## liboqs

### Memory-optimized build option (PR [#2367](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2367))

Bruce will review this PR this week. Basil noted that the MQOM PR ([#2361](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2361)) builds on top of this PR, so the review sequence should be: memory-optimized build option first, then MQOM.

Michael asked how the memory-optimized build option fits with Bruce's OQS Builder work. Bruce clarified: copy-from-upstream remains the supported integration mechanism until OQS Builder is ready, and he will ensure OQS Builder has feature parity with all build options when it is released.

The PR is heading in the right direction but needs someone to work through the remaining CI failures.

### External-μ API for ML-DSA (PR [#2366](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2366))

PR [#2366](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2366) proposes adding support for the external-μ (external mu) entry point of ML-DSA (FIPS 204). Two design approaches were discussed:

1. Introduce a new algorithm identifier (e.g., `MLDSA44_externalmu`) that reuses the existing KeyGen/Sign/Verify API but routes through the external-μ entry point internally.
2. Add a new dedicated API function (e.g., `sign_with_external_mu`), making the distinction explicit at the API level.

Option 1 is implemented in the current PR. Key points from the discussion:

- External-μ appears to be specific to ML-DSA (and Dilithium); there is no reason to expect other algorithms to require this entry point, so a generalized new API is not warranted.
- Bruce raised a concern about user confusion: `MLDSA44_externalmu` and `MLDSA44` would accept the same types of inputs but produce different outputs. The consensus was that, since the algorithm identifiers differ, this is analogous to any two distinct algorithms producing different outputs — and Norman pointed out that in practice the *input* also differs (μ, a pre-computed hash, vs. the raw message). Basil drew an analogy with pre-hashed variants of ML-DSA, which are similarly defined as separate algorithms with their own OIDs.
- Bruce asked about key interoperability: key generation is identical for external-μ and non-external-μ variants, so key pairs could in principle be used interchangeably. Rodrigo and Basil noted that FIPS 204 treats external-μ as an alternative computation path rather than a separate variant, and places no prohibition on key interoperability across the two modes. The liboqs API will be neutral on this question.

**Conclusion:** Proceed with option 1 as implemented in PR [#2366](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2366). Further discussion can continue on the PR.

## OQS-OpenSSL provider

Rodrigo has been working on two recently-opened issues:

- One PR addressing an open issue will be merged after the meeting.
- Issue [#746](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/746) (compare key created from public and private material) has open comments from Rodrigo regarding how behavior should align between OpenSSL's built-in handling of the standardized post-quantum algorithms and the OQS provider; input from contributors is welcome.
- PR [#752](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/752) (Preserve property string for KEMs), submitted by Norm, has a few open questions from Rodrigo; Norm will respond in the PR.

## OQS-OpenSSH

Alex Harrison noted that a small PR will likely be submitted later this week. An engineer at his company used AI-driven testing and found that sending malformed ML-KEM inputs can cause connection closures (not a denial-of-service, but breaks KEM negotiation). The proposed fix adds length verification checks in the ML-KEM code. Alex is still validating the change before submitting the PR.

## TSC chair

Michael raised the question of what the TSC chair role entails in practice, prompted by an observation that external contributors (e.g., from AWS) expect the chair to be the authoritative voice representing the TSC on open community discussions. Some GitHub discussions have stalled waiting for the chair's input.

Douglas described the chair's responsibilities as: chairing TSC meetings, helping move forward open TSC issues and the backlog between meetings, and representing the TSC's view to the wider community. The chair is also free to shape the role as they and the community see fit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Add a job description to issue [#246](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/246) outlining TSC chair responsibilities to help potential candidates understand the commitment and scope of the role.

## Supporting derived keys/seeds in liboqs

Norm raised the topic of adding liboqs API support for deterministic (seed-based) key generation, referring to liboqs issue [#2030](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2030) and a prior strawman proposal.

The proposed approach: add new APIs to the liboqs layer for seed-based key pair derivation; initially set null function pointers for algorithms that do not yet implement it; then populate implementations starting with ML-DSA and expanding to other algorithms as documentation or demand arises.

Key points from the discussion:

- Kamal noted that the underlying functionality likely already exists in upstream implementations but is not currently exposed in the liboqs API.
- FIPS 204 recommends storing seeds rather than expanded keys for ML-DSA. Basil mentioned that IETF key representation drafts are converging on three options: full key, seed only, or both; if both are stored, consistency checks may be needed (though this is above the liboqs layer).
- Rodrigo distinguished two separate concerns that should be addressed independently: (a) deriving a key pair from a seed (new `keypair_from_seed`-style API), and (b) reconstructing a full key pair from a private-key-only representation (as OpenSSL does internally). The logic for (a) would be analogous to what already exists for KEMs.
- Michael recommended designing the API with concrete usage scenarios in mind, and suggested following the existing KEM de-randomized key generation API as closely as possible unless there are specific reasons to differ.
- Douglas echoed this: model the API on the KEM approach, and surface any design differences as explicit discussion points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Norm):** Continue discussion in issue [#2030](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2030), tagging relevant contributors to gather broader input.
